subagent_reports.topic: Validate Temporal/architecture options for optional Advance Epics; discover missed opportunities within proposal scope

sources.label: backlog-coordination spec v1.3.0 (purpose + rq-backlogCoord01)
sources.summary: Explicit precedent: 'no project-level shared workflow'. The change workflow IS the durable claim record. Cross-entity linkage flows through Temporal Visibility search attributes (AdvAffectedProjects, AdvBacklogIssueNumber), not a parent workflow.

sources.label: ADV search-attributes hard cap
sources.summary: Temporal dev server hard-caps custom KeywordList attributes at 3 per namespace. All 3 slots used (AdvAffectedProjects, AdvWorktreeBranches, AdvWorktreePaths). New KeywordList linkage must displace one or encode as single-value Keyword (like AdvBacklogIssueNumber).

sources.label: PSW retirement denylist
sources.summary: A project-singleton workflow (projectWorkflow/PSW) previously held change_summaries/session registry/worktree registry. It was explicitly RETIRED (D3) and is guarded by a denylist test against revival. Change summaries now live in per-change workflow state + ChangeSummaryMemo.

sources.label: changeWorkflow is the only workflow type + uses continue-as-new
sources.summary: Single signal/query-driven changeWorkflow. No child workflows. Already calls wf.continueAsNew after wf.condition(wf.allHandlersFinished) to bound history growth (size-guard tested).

sources.label: fast_follow_of flat lineage metadata
sources.summary: Same-project parent linkage already exists as flat metadata { parent_change_id, linked_at } seeded at create. Validates parent exists. Not a workflow; pure projection field surfaced in status/list with the down-right arrow marker.

sources.label: Visibility-backed change enumeration
sources.summary: client.workflow.list({query}) with AsyncIterable pagination over AdvAffectedProjects + AdvChangeStatus is the canonical 'which changes exist now' source. queryActiveChangesByIssueNumbers uses same path (batched 100/call) for O(1) backlog annotation.

sources.label: Temporal TS canonical entity/continue-as-new pattern
sources.summary: Long-lived entity workflow = signals + setHandler + condition + continueAsNew, re-seeding state across boundary. WorkflowStream/openai-agents samples capture state then continueAsNew when continueAsNewSuggested. Matches ADV's existing changeWorkflow shape.

sources.label: Temporal TS parent/child + ABANDON + typed search attributes
sources.summary: If Epic-as-parent-workflow were chosen, startChild + ParentClosePolicy.ABANDON decouples child lifecycle. Canonical guidance favors avoiding coupling independent long-lived entities as parent/child; prefer ID-based linkage + Visibility queries. Search attributes are the idiomatic cross-entity index.

subagent_reports.architecture_assessment: Two viable Temporal modeling options. OPTION A (boring, recommended): model Epic as flat linkage metadata + optionally a lightweight Epic record, with membership/order resolved by Visibility/ID queries, directly mirroring the existing backlog-coordination precedent ('change workflow IS the claim; no project-level shared workflow') and the fast_follow_of lineage pattern. OPTION B: a dedicated long-lived Epic entity workflow (signals/queries/continue-as-new) holding the ordered roadmap. Temporal supports B canonically, BUT ADV already TRIED-AND-RETIRED a project-singleton workflow (PSW, D3) whose responsibilities (change_summaries, registries) are exactly the parent-container-aggregating-children shape an Epic workflow would reintroduce; a do-not-revive denylist test guards against it. A per-Epic (not per-project) workflow is not identical to PSW but re-opens the same coupling/replay/aggregation risk class the team deliberately exited. Two hard constraints bind any design: (1) the 3-KeywordList search-attribute cap means Epic membership cannot add a new KeywordList without displacing one (encode Epic-id as a single Keyword AdvEpicId like AdvBacklogIssueNumber, or keep membership in flat change metadata resolved via existing AdvAffectedProjects-scoped Visibility queries); (2) the Must-Not constraint forbids moving correctness-critical state into heuristic/prose roadmap text, so ordering/membership must be typed/structural.

validation.notes: Proposal scope is achievable with boring, precedent-aligned Temporal modeling; no architecture blocker. Two design questions need orchestrator/user decision: (Q1) Epic as flat-metadata+Visibility vs dedicated Epic entity workflow (strongly lean flat-metadata to avoid PSW-class revival); (Q2) how Epic-id is indexed given the 3-KeywordList cap. ADV state access via tools succeeded; lgrep semantic search unavailable (Voyage API timeout x3) so used grep/read/spec tools instead (consumer warning).

subagent_reports.recommendation: Discovery agreement should: (1) Adopt the boring linkage model: Epic membership as optional flat change metadata (epic_id + recommended order index) mirroring fast_follow_of, with Epic context/order resolved through existing Visibility queries (AdvAffectedProjects-scoped) rather than a parent workflow, unless discovery proves a stateful Epic workflow is required. (2) If durable Epic narrative/shell-entry state is needed, prefer a single lightweight per-Epic entity workflow ONLY if flat metadata + a small Epic record proves insufficient, and explicitly reconcile against the PSW-retirement do-not-revive guard before committing. (3) Add AC for the 3-KeywordList cap: index Epic-id as a single-value Keyword (AdvEpicId) if cross-session Epic membership queries are required, NOT a new KeywordList. (4) Add AC: ordering + membership are structural/typed (P33), never prose roadmap text. (5) Add AC for failure handling of missing/archived child changes in Epic order. (6) Add AC: shell-entry promotion reuses adv_change_create with epic linkage seeded at create (like origin/fast_follow seeding), not a late disk patch.
follow_ups: Orchestrator must decide Q1 (flat-metadata vs Epic entity workflow) and Q2 (Epic-id indexing under 3-KeywordList cap); surface to user, involves architecture/user-value tradeoff.

Before any Epic-workflow design, re-read the PSW retirement decision (D3) and no-psw-references.test.ts to confirm a per-Epic workflow does not re-trip the do-not-revive guard or its replay/aggregation failure class.
CONSUMER WARNING: lgrep_search_semantic timed out (Voyage API) across 3 attempts; findings used grep/read/adv_spec instead. Re-verify membership-query code paths with semantic search if it recovers.
PACKET NOTE: agreement/AC artifacts not yet formed (discovery gate pending); assessment grounded in approved proposal + code/spec evidence only.

subagent_reports.topic: Design leverage scout for Advance Epics — simplification paths, missed leverage, over-engineering risk

sources.label: 3-KeywordList hard cap + single-Keyword precedent
sources.summary: Temporal dev server hard-caps custom KeywordList at 3/namespace; all 3 used. Draft's single-value AdvEpicId Keyword (mirroring AdvBacklogIssueNumber) is the correct precedent path — BUT single-value Keyword structurally limits a change to exactly ONE epic. Acceptable for v1 (AC8 optional membership) but must be stated as an explicit design invariant.

sources.label: PSW denylist is symbol/name-based, not entity-workflow-based
sources.summary: Denylist bans PSW symbols (projectWorkflow, change_summaries aggregation, registry updates), NOT 'a second workflow type'. A per-epic entity workflow does not trip the guard. The retirement risk is the aggregation/replay risk CLASS (a parent holding ordered child refs + narrative), not the literal symbols.

sources.label: Existing boring durable-record pattern: atomic JSONL projections
sources.summary: ADV already stores typed, durable, append-only records (agenda/wisdom/reflections) under the same external project-id dir via writeJsonlAtomic — no Temporal workflow. Epic narrative + ordered roadmap entries is exactly this low-frequency typed-record shape.

sources.label: changeWorkflow continue-as-new + aggregate size cap cost
sources.summary: Every Temporal entity workflow inherits allHandlersFinished+continueAsNew re-seeding and the 1.8MB aggregate state cap discipline. A per-epic workflow imports all of this machinery for what is a small ordered list + narrative string.

sources.label: fast_follow_of flat-metadata lineage precedent
sources.summary: Same-project parent linkage already exists as flat typed metadata {parent_change_id, linked_at} seeded at create, surfaced via list/status annotation — no workflow. epic_membership projection on child changes can mirror this exactly.

subagent_reports.architecture_assessment: The draft design is achievable and contract-aligned, but it picks the HEAVIER of two viable storage models for the Epic record itself. Two layers are sound and should stay: (1) epic_membership as optional flat projection on child changes (mirrors fast_follow_of) and (2) single-value AdvEpicId Keyword for O(1) Visibility membership lookup (mirrors AdvBacklogIssueNumber, correct under the 3-KeywordList cap). The questionable layer is the DEDICATED PER-EPIC TEMPORAL ENTITY WORKFLOW owning narrative + ordered roadmap. The only concurrency requirement in the contract is AC10 (deterministic error/warning on concurrent reorder/promotion) — satisfiable with optimistic versioning on a typed record, not necessarily a long-lived signal/query workflow. ADV already has a proven, boring, atomic durable-record substrate (JSONL projections: agenda/wisdom/reflections) that fits an Epic's low-write-frequency, single-initiative-edit profile, and it avoids inheriting continue-as-new re-seeding, the 1.8MB aggregate cap discipline, replay-determinism constraints, and the aggregation/replay risk class the team deliberately exited with PSW (D3). A new workflow type also adds a worker-bundle boundary surface and a second history-poisoning replay cliff. This is not a blocker — a per-epic workflow is canonically supported and does not literally trip the PSW denylist — but it is a simplification opportunity the design should explicitly justify or drop before planning.

validation.notes: No correctness blocker. One MAJOR simplification candidate (drop per-epic workflow in favor of typed JSONL/projection record + optimistic versioning) and one invariant to make explicit (single-value AdvEpicId => one epic per change). Both should be surfaced to the user before the design gate closes since storage-model choice was the open Q1 from discovery.

subagent_reports.recommendation: Before completing the design gate: (1) SURFACE TO USER — challenge the dedicated per-epic entity workflow. Default to the boring substrate: store the Epic record (id, title, narrative, ordered entries[], status summary, timestamps, version) as a typed atomic projection reusing the jsonl-atomic-writer pattern, with optimistic version checks to satisfy AC10 concurrent-edit determinism. Adopt a workflow ONLY if design proves a requirement the projection cannot meet (e.g. cross-session signal coordination that Visibility queries + version-CAS cannot provide). (2) AUTO-ADOPT — keep epic_membership as flat optional projection on child changes (mirror fast_follow_of) and AdvEpicId as single-value Keyword; add an explicit design invariant that single-value Keyword => one epic per change in v1 (consistent with AC8). (3) AUTO-ADOPT — promotion must seed epic linkage at adv_change_create time (like origin/fast_follow seeding), never a late disk patch (AC3 idempotency). (4) If the workflow IS retained, the design must include a short written reconciliation against the PSW D3 retirement risk class and confirm the new workflow type passes the workflow-bundle-boundary guard.
follow_ups: Storage-model decision (per-epic workflow vs typed JSONL/projection record) is the surviving open Q1 from discovery and involves an architecture/user-value tradeoff — must be surfaced to user, not auto-adopted.
If per-epic workflow retained: add design note reconciling against PSW retirement (D3) risk class and confirm plugin/src/temporal/workflow-bundle-boundary.test.ts coverage for the new workflow type.
Make 'single-value AdvEpicId => one epic per change in v1' an explicit, tested design invariant.

lgrep_search_semantic not attempted this pass (prior discovery reported Voyage API timeouts); findings used adv tools + grep/read. Re-verify membership-query code paths with semantic search if it recovers.

subagent_reports.topic: Design validation — Advance Epics first-class entity with per-Epic Temporal workflow

sources.label: plugin/src/temporal/search-attributes.ts (lines 5-28)
sources.summary: Confirms Temporal dev-server caps KeywordList at 3 per namespace; all 3 slots consumed (AdvAffectedProjects, AdvWorktreeBranches, AdvWorktreePaths). AdvBacklogIssueNumber is single-value Keyword and does NOT consume a slot — exact precedent the design cites for AdvEpicId.

sources.label: plugin/src/__tests__/no-psw-references.test.ts
sources.summary: PSW retirement denylist targets specific named symbols (buildProjectWorkflowId, getBoundedProjectWorkflowAccess, project-workflow-helper imports), not all per-entity workflows. A distinctly-named epicWorkflow with one-workflow-per-Epic scope does not trip it.

sources.label: plugin/src/types/changes.ts (line 747)
sources.summary: fast_follow_of is an additive optional same-project lineage projection on change state. epic_membership mirrors this proven replay-safe pattern accurately.

sources.label: Temporal Entity Pattern — TypeScript SDK
sources.summary: Canonical Temporal best practice for durable per-entity state: signal/query + condition wait + continue-as-new. Validates epicWorkflow as boring/proven, not novel.

sources.label: Temporal — Handling Signals, Queries & Updates (idempotency + Continue-As-New)
sources.summary: For Signals with Continue-As-New, deduplication MUST be implemented in workflow code via a custom idempotency key; server Update-ID dedup is per-run only. Also: finish all handlers before CAN. Design Decision 3/5 under-specifies cross-CAN signal dedup.

sources.label: advance-meta spec rq-statusCliBridge01 / rq-roadmapCliBridge01 / rq-statusCliWorkerFree01
sources.summary: /adv-status and /adv-roadmap are LAW-bound thin CLI bridges that MUST NOT instruct agent fanout to ADV MCP tools; CLI status reads Visibility worker-free. Design Slice D/E Epic annotation must route through the CLI/Visibility layer, not add agent-side Epic hydration to these bridges.

subagent_reports.architecture_assessment: The design is architecturally sound and strongly aligned with existing ADV patterns. Three load-bearing technical claims verified true against source: (1) the AdvEpicId single-value Keyword choice correctly avoids the genuinely-exhausted 3-slot KeywordList cap, exactly mirroring the AdvBacklogIssueNumber precedent; (2) the per-Epic epicWorkflow does not revive the retired project-shared-workflow — the denylist is symbol-specific and the scope is one-workflow-per-Epic with no project-wide aggregation or child orchestration; (3) epic_membership is a faithful additive-optional projection in the proven fast_follow_of mold. External evidence confirms the entity-workflow (signal/query + continue-as-new) approach is canonical Temporal best practice, satisfying the simplicity bias. The main gap is under-specification, not wrong direction: continue-as-new with signals requires explicit in-workflow idempotency-key dedup and all-handlers-finished draining before CAN, which the design references only loosely. A secondary spec-compliance risk: Slices D/E touch /adv-status and /adv-roadmap which are law-bound CLI bridges forbidding agent fanout — Epic annotation must flow through CLI/Visibility surfaces, not agent-side hydration on those bridges.

validation.notes: No conflicts or contract compromises. Two CAUTION items require planning-phase attention before execution: (1) make continue-as-new signal idempotency/dedup and all-handlers-finished draining an explicit design-derived criterion (currently implied by CAN-seed wording only) — per Temporal docs, server dedup is per-run and breaks across CAN; (2) ensure Slice D/E Epic annotation on /adv-status and /adv-roadmap routes through the worker-free CLI/Visibility bridge per advance-meta rq-statusCliBridge01/rq-roadmapCliBridge01/rq-statusCliWorkerFree01, not agent-side Epic hydration. Both are addressable in design-derived criteria/tests without changing the core architecture.

subagent_reports.recommendation: VALIDATED-with-CAUTION. Proceed to planning. Add two design-derived criteria before execution: (DC-A) epicWorkflow signal handling must be idempotent across continue-as-new via an explicit in-workflow idempotency key and must drain/finish handlers before CAN (cite https://docs.temporal.io/handling-messages); this hardens the existing 'idempotent shell promotion' and 'concurrent reorder CAS' criteria against the CAN boundary. (DC-B) Epic annotation added to /adv-status and /adv-roadmap surfaces must be sourced from the worker-free CLI/Visibility bridge and must not introduce agent fanout, preserving advance-meta CLI-bridge laws. No simpler viable alternative was overlooked — disk/JSONL, project-singleton, flat-metadata, and ROADMAP.md-v2 were each correctly rejected with evidence consistent with the Temporal-only-runtime project context.
follow_ups: Planning: add design-derived criterion for cross-continue-as-new signal idempotency + all-handlers-finished drain on epicWorkflow.
Planning: add criterion/test that Epic annotation on /adv-status and /adv-roadmap respects advance-meta CLI-bridge no-fanout laws and worker-free Visibility read path.
Implementation: confirm worker-bundle boundary test is extended to cover epicWorkflow workflow-safe imports as Slice B states.

subagent_reports.topic: Revised Advance Epics design validation: retrofit linking, unlink/move, product/multi-project membership, cross-project trust, fast_follow_of preservation, no-distributed-transaction model

sources.label: plugin/src/types/epics.ts
sources.summary: Current Epic domain types. EpicChangeEntry holds only change_id:string (L106), no project identity. EpicEntrySchema is a strict discriminatedUnion (L131) with NO .passthrough on member objects; only top-level EpicSchema has .passthrough (L166). EpicMembership projection (L183-194) carries epic_id/entry_id/order/title/linked_at only — no source/repo_id/epic_project_id.

sources.label: plugin/src/tools/epic.ts
sources.summary: Current adv_epic_link_change/unlink_change. link only adds an Epic entry via store.epics.linkChange; it does NOT signal the child change to set epic_membership. unlink only removes the Epic entry; no child projection clear. No target_path, no audit evidence, no expected_version, no move tool — confirming the design's named gap is real.

sources.label: plugin/src/storage/store-temporal/epics.ts
sources.summary: linkChange/unlinkChange fire Epic-workflow signals only (changeLinkedSignal/changeUnlinkedSignal). No cross-store child mutation, no withTargetPathStore routing. Idempotency keys embed new Date().toISOString() (L46-48) so they are time-varying, not deterministic per logical op — weakens dedupe for retried link/move.

sources.label: plugin/src/temporal/epic-state.ts
sources.summary: applyChangeLinkedToState builds a change entry with kind/entry_id/order/change_id only — no change_ref, no membership_status. Pure-function state model is well structured (version CAS, idempotency ledger, ring-buffer rejections) and is a sound base for the proposed audited statuses.

sources.label: plugin/src/types/signals.ts
sources.summary: ChangeLinkedSignalPayload carries entryId/changeId/title/order/idempotencyKey/linkedAt — no project_id/target_path/repo_id, no link_evidence. Design's expanded payload (change_ref + audit) is net-new and additive.

sources.label: plugin/src/tools/target-project.ts
sources.summary: Canonical cross-project trust gate the design reuses. resolveTargetProject: current_project=trusted; related repo=trusted; else mutation requires target_confirmed:true + confirmationEvidence (L158-162). withTargetPathStore enforces Temporal queue serviceability before mutation and throws TargetProjectError when not serviceable (L318-322) — directly supports the design's TARGET_UNREACHABLE / TARGET_CONFIRMATION_REQUIRED model.

sources.label: plugin/src/temporal/search-attributes.ts + change membership wiring
sources.summary: AdvEpicId single-value Keyword derives from state.epic_membership.epic_id. epic_membership is ONLY set at change creation via initialMetadata and seeded into workflow state. There is NO epicMembershipSet/Cleared change-workflow signal anywhere (grep returned none) — confirming retrofit projection onto an existing change is genuinely unimplemented and is the core new work.

sources.label: plugin/src/tools/change.ts product scope_repos
sources.summary: resolveScopeRepos + productContext already implement product repo identity (repo_id/repo_project_id/role/required, duplicate validation, single_repo fallback). Design's epic_scope.repos shape reuses this exact identity model, so multi-project Epic scope is grounded in an existing, proven pattern (supports DONT8).

subagent_reports.architecture_assessment: The revised design is structurally sound and well-aligned with the actual codebase. It correctly diagnoses the real V1 gap (adv_epic_link_change updates the Epic entry list but never projects epic_membership onto the child change, and there is no change-workflow signal to do so), and it reuses proven existing patterns rather than inventing parallel mechanisms: (1) the cross-project trust/confirmation gate and queue-serviceability check in target-project.ts map 1:1 onto the design's TARGET_CONFIRMATION_REQUIRED / TARGET_UNREACHABLE codes; (2) the product scope_repos / productContext identity shape in change.ts is the same identity model the design adopts for epic_scope.repos; (3) AdvEpicId single-value Keyword + Epic-entry-as-source-of-truth correctly avoids relying on one project's Visibility for all repos. Most importantly, the design explicitly rejects false distributed-transaction semantics across the independent Epic and change workflows and instead specifies a best-effort two-phase flow with explicit membership_status (projection_pending/target_unreachable/etc.), deterministic idempotency keys, and an adv_epic_repair_membership recovery tool — this is the correct structural-correctness posture for two independent workflows and matches the existing rejection/idempotency-ledger machinery in epic-state.ts. fast_follow_of preservation (C8/DONT7) is honored: epic_membership is a separate projection object and the design never reparents fast_follow_of. The design is implementable on the current foundation with additive schema/signal changes; nothing in it contradicts the layer boundaries or the signal/query-only workflow surface. Remaining risks are migration-detail and idempotency-determinism concerns, not architectural defects.

validation.notes: No blocking architectural defects. Design can proceed to planning. Non-blocking adjustments below should be folded into the plan as explicit tasks/acceptance items so they are not lost: (1) IDEMPOTENCY DETERMINISM — current idempotencyKey() in store-temporal/epics.ts:46-48 appends new Date().toISOString(), making keys time-varying; the design's link/move/repair idempotency and 'retry is idempotent by deterministic key {from,to,change,entry}' claim requires switching to deterministic logical keys (no wall-clock component) or the two-phase retry guarantee is not actually achieved. Make this an explicit planning task. (2) ENTRY SCHEMA MIGRATION — EpicEntrySchema is a STRICT discriminatedUnion (epics.ts:131) with no .passthrough on member objects; only top-level EpicSchema passes through. Replacing change_id:string with a nested change_ref object is a breaking parse change. The design's 'interpreted as same-project refs during migration/read normalization' must be specified concretely: either keep change_id as an optional legacy field alongside change_ref, or add an explicit normalization step + parse tests for pre-existing entries. (3) CHILD-PROJECTION SIGNAL — the new epicMembershipSetSignal/epicMembershipClearedSignal must route through fireSignalAndRefresh (AGENTS.md rq-cacheRefresh01) and, for cross-project, through the target store cache, mirroring task mutation (rq-crossProjectTaskMutation01); call this out so the projection write obeys cache-refresh discipline. (4) UNLINK AUDIT/EVIDENCE — design says unlink_evidence required but current unlink tool has no evidence/version args; ensure planning adds audited unlink while preserving the existing missing-entry idempotent tolerance in applyChangeUnlinkedToState. (5) ONE-EPIC-PER-CHANGE ENFORCEMENT — design relies on reading target epic_membership to reject CHANGE_ALREADY_IN_EPIC; for cross-project targets this read may be unreachable, so the plan should define behavior when the target cannot be read (fail-closed vs projection_pending) to avoid silent double-membership.

subagent_reports.recommendation: Proceed to the planning gate. Verdict CAUTION (proceed-with-conditions): the architecture is correct and grounded in existing patterns, but planning MUST encode five non-blocking items as explicit tasks/acceptance criteria before execution: (a) deterministic (wall-clock-free) idempotency keys for link/unlink/move/repair; (b) concrete backward-compat normalization for EpicChangeEntry change_id→change_ref with parse tests, since the entry union is strict; (c) new epicMembershipSet/Cleared change-workflow signals routed via fireSignalAndRefresh and target-store cache for cross-project; (d) audited unlink with evidence/expected_version while keeping idempotent missing-entry tolerance; (e) defined fail-closed behavior for one-Epic-per-change when the target change is unreachable. The no-distributed-transaction / membership_status + repair model is the right call and should be kept as-is.
follow_ups: Planning: add task to convert idempotencyKey() in store-temporal/epics.ts to deterministic logical keys (drop new Date().toISOString() component) for link/unlink/move/repair retry safety.
Planning: add backward-compat normalization + parse tests for EpicChangeEntry change_id→change_ref (strict discriminatedUnion, not passthrough).
Planning: specify cross-project one-Epic-per-change behavior when target change read is unreachable (fail-closed vs projection_pending).
Note: full ADV artifact bodies (problem-statement/proposal raw) were marked readable:false; validation used adv_change_show include flags and source code only, per ADV state access policy.

wisdom.content: Epic workflows can be one-per-entity without reviving the retired project shared workflow pattern, but design/tests must make the boundary explicit: no project singleton aggregation, no child workflow orchestration, workflow-safe imports only, and Visibility/query surfaces for cross-change lookup.

For cross-change Epic membership lookup, mirror the backlog claim pattern: store a compact optional projection on each child change, index it via a single-value Temporal Keyword (`AdvEpicId`), and query Visibility by project + Epic ID rather than hydrating all changes.

Adding new MCP tools requires updating not only tool implementation/registry but also frozen tool-name snapshots and CLI surface matrix coverage; otherwise asset/bridge contract tests fail even when tool behavior is correct.

When using repo-local `bin/oc-test targeted`, run it from the repo root and pass plugin test filters as `src/...` paths because the wrapper invokes Vitest with `plugin/` as cwd. Passing `plugin/src/...` yields 'No test files found'.

Epic store reads must not auto-start missing Epic workflows: auto-start hides stale child links and missing-Epic recovery bugs. Create should eagerly start, but get/list should return null for missing workflows and mutations should emit typed `epic_not_found`.

When adding a new ADV workflow concept, update orchestration allowlists, typed-worker packet guidance, command context-loading instructions, CLI/asset contract tests, and source-of-truth docs together; otherwise runtime behavior and agent contracts drift.

Adding or reordering ADV Temporal search attributes requires updating all pinned test fixtures in `service.test.ts`, `service-reconnect.test.ts`, and `observability.test.ts`; full-suite failures can also surface stale exact spec-version pins and missing rq-* citation coverage after spec bumps.

When adding backward-compatible schemas that make a formerly-required field optional (legacy `change_id` plus new `change_ref`), update hot-path formatters and type guards in the same task; TypeScript will surface old assumptions as `string | undefined`, and public schema artifacts must be regenerated before schema-registry tests pass.

For change-workflow projection signals, keep the full contract aligned in one slice: signal payload schema, signal-name constant, client message binding, workflow local binding/handler, pure state helper, message-surface fixture, and search-attribute tests. Existing signalMutation success path already upserts AdvEpicId when state.epic_membership changes.

Same-project Epic link/unlink/move must mutate the child change through Store.changes APIs, not only the Epic workflow. Keeping Epic entry state and child epic_membership projection in sync requires explicit expected identity checks on clear/move; otherwise unlink can erase the wrong projection or move can silently overwrite another Epic membership.

Cross-project Epic membership routing should keep the Epic workflow owner-local and route only child projection mutations through `withTargetPathStore`; link/move entries need explicit `changeProjectId`, `repoId`, and `targetPath` refs so the owner Epic can represent product-scope membership without mutating untrusted targets silently.

Epic repair/status surface work needs both mutation and presentation paths in one verified slice: member_status mapping in map/compact output, workflow/store membership-status signal, tool registry/allowlist/title surfaces, and targeted tests for repair modes plus CLI bridge contract.

Spec/docs asset tasks can look green before new scope is actually locked: update both .adv/specs JSON and docs/specs markdown, then strengthen asset tests to assert the new rq-* IDs and ADV_INSTRUCTIONS/tool-matrix wording so future changes cannot drift silently.

Epic integrated verification can be bounded but high-confidence by combining the Epic-focused targeted matrix (types, state, workflow, visibility, store, tools, assets, registry), `pnpm run check`, and repo-local `bin/oc-test full`; this catches both focused retrofit regressions and wider repo contract drift.

documents.proposal:
# Add Advance Epics

## Why

Large ADV initiatives often exceed one change, but ADV has no first-class parent surface to hold shared initiative context, sequence related changes, and guide next work. Current project-level roadmap state is brittle and too broad, while individual changes can become oversized or lose their larger purpose.

## What Changes

Advance should gain optional **Epics**: parent planning containers for multi-change initiatives. An Epic should carry narrative context and its own sequenced roadmap of existing ADV changes plus future shell entries.

V1 should focus on scoped initiative context and recommended ordering, not full project-management behavior.

## User Outcomes

- Users can understand which related ADV changes belong to a larger initiative and why the sequence matters.
- Users can choose the next meaningful change inside an initiative without relying on a brittle project-level roadmap.
- Users can split oversized work into multiple ADV changes without losing shared context.
- Agents can resume or start related work with the Epic goal, current sequence, and future-work hints in context.
- Future work can be captured as lightweight shell entries before it is ready for full ADV proposal/discovery.

## Affected Code

Likely affected areas, to verify during discovery:

- ADV change/state model and typed schemas.
- Temporal-backed persistence/query surfaces for initiative-level state.
- Change creation/resume/status/read tools that need to expose or consume Epic context.
- Roadmap/backlog coordination surfaces currently centered on GitHub Project / ROADMAP snapshots.
- Command contracts and agent context-loading rules.
- Specs and documentation for any new Epic capability.

Initial quick checks found existing same-project fast-follow lineage (`fast_follow_of.parent_change_id`) and existing roadmap/backlog tooling, but no first-class Epic capability/spec.

## Related Repositories

Current repo only: `advance`. No product-linked repo config was detected in the root project config. Cross-repo Epic behavior, if needed, should be validated during discovery rather than assumed in proposal.

## Constraints

- Epic membership must be optional; not every ADV change needs an Epic.
- Epic order should be a recommended sequence in v1, not a hard gate.
- Shell entries need at least a title plus rough acceptance/success hint, but do not require full ADV agreement upfront.
- V1 should prioritize readable hierarchy/order and agent context over rich UI.
- Temporal/source-of-truth model must be validated before implementation design.
- Failure handling for Epic creation, update, shell promotion, and missing/archived child changes must be defined before implementation.

## Impact

- Reduces pressure to make one ADV change carry an entire initiative.
- Gives users and agents a scoped planning surface for initiative-level context.
- May replace or demote project-level roadmap usage for ADV-owned initiative planning.
- Requires careful source-of-truth design because existing backlog/roadmap behavior uses GitHub Project and snapshot mirrors while in-flight ADV state lives in Temporal.

## Context

The current system has related primitives but no Epic-level parent:

- `fast_follow_of.parent_change_id` records same-project child lineage for follow-up changes.
- `adv_roadmap` / backlog coordination read GitHub Project and `.adv/roadmap-snapshot.json` as ranked backlog sources.
- Existing guidance keeps ranked backlog outside Temporal, while in-flight ADV state and linkage live with ADV state.

Advance Epics should be explored as a scoped planning/context layer, not as a replacement for every external backlog or GitHub issue workflow.

## Discovery Agenda

### Codebase unknowns

- Where Epic state should live: separate Epic workflow, project-level workflow, change workflow metadata, or another Temporal-backed structure.
- How existing fast-follow lineage should relate to Epic membership.
- How `adv_change_show`, status, roadmap/backlog tools, and resume/apply flows should load Epic context.
- Whether a new `advance-epics` spec is needed or existing `advance-workflow` / `backlog-coordination` specs should expand.

### Ecosystem unknowns

- Temporal modeling tradeoffs for parent containers that order many child workflows.
- Best UX pattern for a terminal/agent-readable initiative roadmap without becoming a full project-management board.

### Domain unknowns

- Exact promotion behavior for shell entries when they become ADV changes.
- How much rough AC is enough for shell entries before discovery begins.
- Whether archived/closed changes remain visible in Epic history by default.

### Integration unknowns

- How Epics should coexist with GitHub Project, ROADMAP.md, issue-origin claims, and triage.
- Whether Epic context should influence change selection recommendations, archive output, or acceptance summaries.
- How cross-repo/product-linked initiatives should be represented if later needed.
- What error, recovery, and rollback behavior is needed when Epic membership, ordering, or shell promotion fails.

## Scope

### In Scope

- Define Epic as optional parent context for multi-change initiatives.
- Define an Epic-local ordered roadmap containing linked ADV changes and shell entries.
- Define minimum shell-entry content as title plus rough acceptance/success hint.
- Define how users and agents discover current Epic context and next recommended work.
- Define how oversized changes can become children of an Epic without losing context.
- Determine whether new specs, schemas, tools, or command updates are required.

### Out of Scope

- Mandatory Epic membership for all changes.
- Hard-blocking dependency graph semantics in v1.
- Full Jira-like project management: assignments, estimates, boards, sprints, or ownership workflows.
- GitHub Projects clone or replacement for all external stakeholder backlog use.
- Rich visual UI beyond a clear text/terminal representation unless discovery proves it necessary.

### Must Not

- Must not make Epic order a hard gate in v1.
- Must not require shell entries to complete full ADV proposal/discovery upfront.
- Must not move correctness-critical ADV state into heuristic/prose-only roadmap text.
- Must not duplicate existing roadmap/backlog claim logic without a clear source-of-truth boundary.

# Discovery Findings

## Discovery Checklist

| Step | Result | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Skill Discovery | PASS | Considered architecture/opportunity skills; no pending skill review found. `adv-opportunity-scout` applied through researcher. |
| Prior Research Extension | PASS | `docs/repo-improve-prep.md` cited; no Epic-specific prior research found. New finding: summary/status hot paths and search-attribute constraints matter for Epic context loading. |
| Conflict & Related-Work Scan | PASS | No active duplicate Epic change found. Related broad scope/status/roadmap agenda items exist but do not own Epic scope. |
| Edge Case Investigation | PASS | Edge cases captured below. |
| Design Question Depth | PASS | State ownership, linkage, shell promotion, visual surfacing, and roadmap coexistence questions annotated below. |
| Draft Spec Delta Shapes | PASS | Draft `rq-epic*` deltas captured below. |
| Related Pattern Scan | PASS | Similar patterns found: fast-follow lineage, ops follow-up links, backlog issue Visibility annotation, status context enrichment. |
| LBP Check | PASS | Evidence favors typed optional linkage and Visibility-style indexes; implementation storage remains design-phase. |
| Completeness Verification | PASS | Problem and solution scope broad enough; no sole-entry claim accepted without design-phase verification. |

## Skills Considered

- `adv-arch-detection`: relevant to architecture inconsistency risk; not loaded separately because direct code/spec scan plus researcher covered source-of-truth decisions.
- `adv-opportunity-scout`: relevant; applied by researcher during discovery.
- `adv-improve`: relevant for broader external UX research; not required before agreement because v1 excludes project-manager/GitHub clone behavior.
- `lgrep`: used where available; semantic search timed out, so source evidence came from symbol/text searches and direct reads.

## Extends

- `docs/repo-improve-prep.md`: prior performance research. Relevant findings: `adv_status` default path currently hydrates broad status data; read-path latency and Visibility/search-attribute choices affect any Epic context loaded into status/change-list surfaces. New finding: Epic context must be designed as a bounded include or summary projection so it does not worsen hot-path status latency.
- No `temp/*.md` brainstorm docs found.
- No Epic-specific archived/prior change found.

## Conflict Scan

- Active changes: no obvious duplicate for Advance Epics.
- Related but non-overlapping: `tightenAdvScopeDiscipline`, stale status/roadmap/cleanup agenda items, and performance research around status/list read paths.
- Validation: current change passes strict validation; pre-discovery `NO_TASKS` and `NO_DELTAS` warnings are expected.
- Agenda: many generic follow-ups; no direct Epic owner found in returned pending set.

## Current State

- No first-class Epic concept exists in code or specs.
- Change workflows are signal/query state holders. `changeWorkflow` owns state, gates, tasks, artifacts, and continues-as-new to bound history.
- Same-project fast-follow lineage exists as `fast_follow_of.parent_change_id` and is seeded at change creation.
- Ops follow-up links demonstrate richer parent/child traceability with parent outbound links and child profiles.
- Backlog coordination uses GitHub Project / snapshot for ranked backlog and Temporal Visibility for active-change annotation by issue number.
- Search attributes are already constrained: Temporal dev server caps custom `KeywordList` attributes at 3, and all three are used. Single-value `Keyword` attributes like `AdvBacklogIssueNumber` remain viable.
- Status/context snapshots are compact and budgeted; Epic context must fit bounded output or use explicit include/detail views.

## Edge Cases

1. Missing Epic while child change references it.
   - Child resume should show recoverable missing-context warning, not crash.
   - Validation should distinguish deleted/archived/missing Epic from no Epic membership.
2. Shell promotion failure.
   - Promotion must be idempotent if change creation succeeds but Epic linkage update fails.
   - Re-running promotion must not create duplicate changes or duplicate shell rows.
3. Archived/closed child changes.
   - Epic history may need completed rows even when default active-change queries omit terminal changes.
   - Next-work selection should ignore terminal rows unless explicitly showing history.
4. Reordering concurrency.
   - Two agents may reorder or promote shells concurrently; source of truth must preserve deterministic order and reject/merge conflicts structurally.
   - Recommended order must not become a hidden hard blocker.
5. Hot-path bloat.
   - Loading full Epic docs on every status/change-list call could regress latency.
   - Summary views need bounded Epic labels/order hints; full context should be opt-in or context-specific.

## Open Design Questions

1. Epic state ownership.
   - Trust model: agent-resolved with code/spec evidence.
   - Blast radius: wrong choice can revive project-workflow coupling or make shell state non-durable.
   - Alternatives: separate `epicWorkflow`; typed change metadata plus Visibility; external projection; project workflow. Project workflow is disfavored by existing no-PSW precedent. Final storage model belongs in design.
2. Epic membership vs fast-follow lineage.
   - Trust model: agent-resolved unless user wants different semantics.
   - Blast radius: confusing grouping with parent/child lineage can break recommendations and cleanup.
   - Alternatives: allow both independently; make Epic the only parent; make fast-follow a special Epic row. Evidence favors orthogonal concepts.
3. Shell entry promotion.
   - Trust model: joint: user defines expected UX, agent defines implementation.
   - Blast radius: duplicate changes, stale shell entries, or lost rough AC.
   - Alternatives: replace shell row with change link; keep shell row as historical provenance; create linked change while retaining shell notes.
4. Roadmap coexistence.
   - Trust model: joint because it changes user workflow.
   - Blast radius: over-deprecating project roadmap can lose public/stakeholder backlog semantics.
   - Alternatives: Epics replace ADV-owned initiative planning only; Epics replace all roadmap use; Epics coexist as an overlay.
5. Visual surface.
   - Trust model: joint because this affects UX expectation.
   - Blast radius: too little visual structure fails user need; too much becomes project-manager clone.
   - Alternatives: terminal table/tree; richer chat block; generated doc; later UI/board.

## Draft Spec Deltas

- `rq-epicSchema01`
  - Given an Epic exists, When read through ADV tools, Then it exposes typed id, title, narrative context, ordered entries, and timestamps.
- `rq-epicChangeLink01`
  - Given a change belongs to an Epic, When change context is shown, Then the Epic label/order/context summary is visible without requiring direct state-file reads.
- `rq-epicShellPromotion01`
  - Given a shell entry with title and rough success hint, When it is promoted, Then exactly one ADV change is created or linked and the shell entry records promotion provenance.
- `rq-epicRecommendedOrder01`
  - Given entries are ordered, When a later entry is started first, Then the system may warn but must not block solely due to Epic order.
- `rq-epicRoadmapBoundary01`
  - Given project backlog/roadmap data exists, When Epic roadmap is displayed, Then it does not duplicate GitHub Project/ROADMAP claim semantics or treat prose roadmap text as correctness-critical state.
- `rq-epicVisibility01`
  - Given many changes exist, When listing Epic members, Then lookup uses a bounded typed index/query path and does not require hydrating all changes in hot paths.

## Related Pattern Scan

- Fast-follow lineage: same-project parent metadata seeded at create and surfaced in status/list responses.
- Ops follow-up links: bidirectional traceability model with parent outbound edge and child profile.
- Backlog issue claims: `AdvBacklogIssueNumber` single-value Visibility index, pre-create claim checks, and active annotation path.
- Worktree ownership: Visibility queries find cross-change owners without project-level workflow.
- Context snapshots: pure formatter with bounded lines; full data must be passed in, not fetched from utility layer.

## Completeness Verification

- Problem-completeness: high confidence. Discovery considered user pain, current ADV state, roadmap/backlog source-of-truth, context-loading surfaces, shell entries, ordering, and visual/readability needs.
- Solution-scope: medium-high confidence. V1 scope is clear enough for agreement: optional Epics, ordered entries, shell promotion, context loading, no hard gate. Exact Temporal storage model remains design-phase.
- Sole-entry claim status: no sole-entry implementation claim accepted. Design must verify all creation/linking/status surfaces before implementation.
- Secondary surfaces: command contracts, CLI/status, change-list/show, backlog/roadmap, cleanup/archive/history, and docs/specs are all in-scope for design consideration.

## LBP Check

Evidence-backed direction:

- Keep Epic membership typed and optional.
- Use structural schemas/signals/queries; do not encode correctness in prose roadmap text.
- Avoid project-level shared workflow revival.
- Prefer bounded Visibility-style indexes for membership discovery if Epic linkage must be queried across changes.
- Keep ordering advisory in v1.
- Add a new `advance-epics` capability spec, with additive requirements in `advance-workflow` / `advance-meta` only where existing surfaces are affected.

Implementation storage remains an open design question. Research found two viable directions:

- Typed metadata + Visibility index: low risk, aligns with existing backlog/fast-follow patterns, but must still answer where durable narrative/shell entries live.
- Separate Epic workflow: natural for durable narrative and ordered shell state, but must avoid project-workflow coupling, history growth, and parent/child lifecycle coupling.

## Recommended Objectives

1. Define optional Advance Epics as durable initiative containers for related ADV changes and shell entries.
2. Provide an Epic-local recommended sequence that helps users/agents choose next work without hard-blocking later work.
3. Preserve Epic context in change resume/show/status paths through bounded, typed context loading.
4. Support shell entries with title plus rough success hint and safe promotion into full ADV changes.
5. Keep roadmap/backlog source-of-truth boundaries clear: Epics are ADV initiative planning, not a GitHub Project clone.
6. Define failure/recovery behavior for missing Epics, duplicate promotion, archived children, concurrent edits, and stale links.

## AMBIGUITY ANALYSIS

Coverage: B:C F:C S:P M:P

- S1 MEDIUM Completion Signals
  - Evidence: `V1 should prioritize readable hierarchy/order and agent context over rich UI.`
  - Reason: readable hierarchy is directional but not yet measurable; agreement criteria should define observable displays and context-loading signals.
- M1 MEDIUM Missing Information
  - Evidence: `Exact promotion behavior for shell entries when they become ADV changes.`
  - Reason: shell promotion UX affects acceptance criteria and needs user resolution.
- M2 MEDIUM Missing Information
  - Evidence: `Whether archived/closed changes remain visible in Epic history by default.`
  - Reason: history display affects user expectations and next-work selection.

No CRITICAL findings. No HIGH findings.

## Discovery Opportunity Scout

- Trigger decision: run. Rationale: strategic architecture/product workflow change with broad ADV context impact.
- Candidates considered: 5.
- Auto-adopted/design-around: typed optional membership; single-value Epic index if Visibility is used; seed linkage structurally; reuse batched Visibility patterns.
- Surfaced to design: per-Epic entity workflow vs metadata/index model because storage model is implementation-significant and carries replay/source-of-truth risk.
documents.problemStatement: Large ADV initiatives often start as one or more ordinary changes before the larger initiative boundary is recognized. The current Epic design assumes mostly create-first/current-repo membership, which fails real use: agents need to create an Epic later, link existing changes into it, and track related work across multiple repositories/projects without losing typed state, provenance, or safety boundaries.

# Agreement

## Discovery Update

During acceptance preparation for `addAdvanceEpics`, real use exposed two missing requirements:

1. Epics cannot be create-first only. Users may start one or more ADV changes, then realize those changes belong under a new Epic.
2. Epics cannot be repo-local only for product work. Some Epics must span multiple ADV projects/repositories while still preserving safe target-project mutation rules.

## Revised Objectives

1. Define optional Advance Epics as durable initiative containers for related ADV changes and shell entries.
2. Make Epics the primary ADV planning surface for initiative-level work, replacing project-level ROADMAP.md for that purpose.
3. Support linking existing changes into an Epic after both already exist.
4. Support product/multi-project Epics that can include members from more than one ADV project/repository.
5. Provide an Epic-local recommended sequence that helps users and agents choose next work without hard-blocking later work.
6. Preserve compact Epic context in change resume/show/status paths so agents understand goal, order, and why current work matters.
7. Support shell entries with title plus rough success/AC hint and safe promotion into full ADV changes.
8. Keep roadmap/backlog source-of-truth boundaries explicit while shifting ADV initiative planning from project ROADMAP.md to Epics.
9. Define deterministic failure/recovery behavior for missing Epics, stale child links, duplicate promotion, archived children, concurrent edits, stale order, unreachable target projects, and cross-project link drift.

## Revised Success Criteria

1. Users can create an Epic before or after related changes exist.
2. Users can link existing ADV changes into an Epic without manually editing state or recreating changes.
3. Product initiatives can have one Epic that spans multiple repositories/projects when the product config trusts those repos or the user explicitly confirms the target mutation.
4. Agents can resume a child change with enough Epic context to understand goal, order, repo scope, and why current work matters.
5. Large initiatives can be represented as multiple linked ADV changes without losing shared context.
6. Future work can live as lightweight shell entries until ready for full ADV proposal/discovery.

## Revised Acceptance Criteria

1. Epic record supports title, narrative context, ordered roadmap entries, status/progress summary, timestamps, and repo/project scope metadata.
2. Epic roadmap supports both linked ADV changes and shell entries; every shell entry has title plus rough success/AC hint.
3. Promoting a shell entry replaces that shell row with exactly one linked ADV change row and carries shell title/success hint forward.
4. Existing ADV changes can be linked into an Epic after creation through an audited tool path; linking records Epic ID, entry ID, order, repo/project identity, linked timestamp, and actor/evidence.
5. Existing ADV changes can be unlinked or moved between Epics only through an audited tool path; stale projections are cleaned up or surfaced as recoverable warnings.
6. Same-project linked child changes expose compact `epic_membership` context in change show/status/resume surfaces.
7. Cross-project linked child changes expose compact Epic context when the target project is reachable; when unreachable, source Epic still shows typed stale/unreachable status instead of failing the whole Epic view.
8. Product/multi-project Epics can include entries from at least two configured/trusted repositories without duplicating the Epic as separate repo-local parents.
9. Cross-project Epic membership mutations require the same target-path trust/confirmation safety model as other cross-project ADV mutations.
10. Epic order is advisory: starting or resuming a later entry may warn, but must not block solely due to order.
11. Default Epic view shows compact history for archived/closed child changes and clearly distinguishes next active/future work.
12. Change context/status surfaces show compact Epic membership/context when a change belongs to an Epic.
13. ADV next-work selection can operate from Epics without requiring project-level ROADMAP.md.
14. Epic membership remains optional; non-Epic changes continue existing flow.
15. V1 does not add Jira-like assignment/estimate/sprint workflows or clone GitHub Projects.
16. Missing Epic, stale child link, duplicate promotion, concurrent reorder/promotion, duplicate link, and cross-project target-unavailable paths produce deterministic errors or recoverable warnings.

## Constraints

1. Epic membership is optional.
2. Epic order is advisory in v1 and must not become a hidden hard gate.
3. Shell entries require title plus rough success/AC hint, not full ADV agreement upfront.
4. Correctness-critical Epic state must be structural and typed, not prose-only roadmap text.
5. Hot-path status/change-list output must remain bounded; full Epic context should be explicit or context-specific.
6. Temporal/search-attribute constraints must be respected, including current custom search-attribute limits.
7. Multi-project Epics must respect product config and target-path trust rules; no silent mutation of untrusted projects.
8. Retroactive linking must not rewrite historical `fast_follow_of` lineage; Epic membership is separate initiative membership, not parentage history.

## Avoidances

1. Do not make every change belong to an Epic.
2. Do not add Jira-like assignments, estimates, boards, sprints, or ownership workflows.
3. Do not clone GitHub Projects.
4. Do not hard-block later Epic entries solely because earlier entries are incomplete.
5. Do not require shell entries to complete full ADV proposal/discovery before promotion.
6. Do not manually edit ADV state to link or reparent changes.
7. Do not overload `fast_follow_of` for retroactive Epic membership.
8. Do not require separate duplicate Epics per repository when one product Epic should own the initiative.

## Design Direction to Validate Next

- Keep `fast_follow_of` as historical creation lineage only.
- Add explicit Epic membership/link tools for after-the-fact linking, unlinking, moving, and cross-project membership.
- Represent multi-project Epic scope structurally using product-linked repo/project identity.
- Store source-of-truth membership in the Epic workflow and project compact membership projections onto child changes when reachable.
- Use typed stale/unreachable member status when cross-project children cannot be queried.

## Sign-Off Needed

Discovery is reopened. Approve this revised agreement to proceed into revised design and planning.

# Revised Design: Advance Epics Retrofit + Multi-Project Membership

## Context

The first implementation already added a first-class Epic entity, shell entries, shell promotion, `epic_membership` projection, `AdvEpicId`, and `adv_epic_link_change` / `adv_epic_unlink_change` tools. Real use exposed a design gap: linking an existing change currently updates the Epic entry list but does not make the child change structurally belong to the Epic, and the model is repo-local.

This revision keeps the good V1 foundation and extends it with audited membership projection and product/multi-project semantics.

## Core Principles

1. `fast_follow_of` remains creation lineage only. It is not reparented, rewritten, or overloaded for Epics.
2. Epic membership is initiative membership. It can be added after change creation and moved/unlinked through audited tools.
3. Epic workflow owns membership source-of-truth.
4. Child changes carry compact membership projections only when reachable and safely mutable.
5. Cross-project membership uses the same trust/confirmation model as other target-path ADV mutations.
6. Product Epics are one logical Epic spanning repo entries, not one duplicated repo-local Epic per project.
7. Stale/unreachable target projects degrade member status; they do not make the whole Epic unreadable.

## Domain Model Changes

### Epic Scope

Add scope metadata to Epic state:

```ts
epic_scope: {
  kind: 'repo' | 'product';
  owner_project_id: string;
  owner_repo_id?: string;
  repos: Array<{
    repo_id: string;
    repo_project_id: string;
    path?: string;
    role: 'primary' | 'secondary';
    required: boolean;
  }>;
}
```

- `repo`: current behavior; entries are expected in one project unless explicitly target-linked.
- `product`: entries may belong to any configured product repo.
- Scope is immutable in V1 after Epic creation except additive repo metadata repair by audited update if needed.

### Epic Entry Change Reference

Change entries need project identity, not just `change_id`:

```ts
EpicChangeEntry = {
  kind: 'change';
  entry_id: string;
  order: number;
  change_ref: {
    change_id: string;
    project_id: string;
    repo_id?: string;
    target_path?: string;
  };
  title: string;
  membership_status:
    | 'linked'
    | 'projection_pending'
    | 'projection_stale'
    | 'target_unreachable'
    | 'unlinked'
    | 'terminal';
  linked_at: string;
  linked_by: string;
  link_evidence: string;
  terminal_summary?: { status: 'archived' | 'closed'; completed_at: string };
  promotion?: ShellPromotionProvenance;
}
```

Backward compatibility: existing `change_id` entries must parse and normalize as same-project refs during migration/read normalization. Implementation must not switch the discriminated union in a way that rejects already-created Epic entries.

### Child Projection

Extend `epic_membership` on changes:

```ts
epic_membership: {
  epic_id: string;
  entry_id: string;
  order: number;
  title: string;
  linked_at: string;
  epic_project_id: string;
  repo_id?: string;
  source: 'create' | 'promote_shell' | 'link_existing' | 'move';
}
```

Projection remains single-object in V1: one Epic per change. `AdvEpicId` remains single-value Keyword.

## Tool/API Changes

### Create Epic

`adv_epic_create` gains optional scope fields:

- `scope_kind?: 'repo' | 'product'`
- `scope_repos?: ChangeRepoScope[]`

Defaults:

- single repo project: `repo`
- product-linked project: `product` only when explicitly requested; otherwise current repo

### Link Existing Change

Revise `adv_epic_link_change`:

Inputs:

- `epic_id`
- `change_id`
- `title?` (default from change title when reachable)
- `entry_id?`
- `order?`
- `target_path?`
- `target_confirmed?`
- `confirmationEvidence?`
- `linked_by?` default `agent`
- `link_evidence` required for audited retrofit membership
- `expected_version?` recommended for optimistic concurrency

Behavior:

1. Load Epic from owner project.
2. Resolve target change:
   - no `target_path`: same project store
   - with `target_path`: `withTargetPathStore` using temporal-required target mutation rules
3. Validate target change exists and is non-phantom.
4. Validate one-Epic-per-change: if target `epic_membership` exists for a different Epic, return `CHANGE_ALREADY_IN_EPIC` with current membership and hint to use move.
5. Signal Epic workflow to add entry with typed `change_ref`, audit metadata, and status `projection_pending`.
6. Signal target change workflow to set `epic_membership` projection and refresh search attributes (`AdvEpicId`). Same-project and target-project mutations must use cache-refresh discipline (`fireSignalAndRefresh` or equivalent target-store helper) so read-after-write shows the membership.
7. Signal Epic workflow to mark entry `linked` after projection succeeds.
8. If Epic signal succeeds but target projection fails due target unavailable/poisoned state, keep entry with `projection_pending` or `target_unreachable` and surface recovery action. Do not silently claim full linkage.

Atomicity model: best-effort two-phase with explicit compensating status. Since Epic and change workflows are independent, do not pretend distributed transaction semantics. Correctness is structural through status + idempotency + repair.

### Unlink Existing Change

Revise `adv_epic_unlink_change`:

Inputs:

- `epic_id`
- `entry_id` or `change_id + target_path`
- `target_path?`
- `unlink_evidence` required
- `expected_version?`

Behavior:

1. Resolve Epic entry and target change ref.
2. Clear child `epic_membership` only if it matches `{ epic_id, entry_id }`; mismatch returns `PROJECTION_MISMATCH` and leaves state unchanged unless repair mode is explicitly selected.
3. Remove or mark Epic entry as `unlinked` depending on command option. V1 default: remove from active roadmap; full audit remains in Epic event/history, not default view.
4. Refresh target search attributes to remove `AdvEpicId`.
5. If target unreachable, mark Epic entry `projection_stale`/`target_unreachable` and surface repair action.

### Move Between Epics

Add `adv_epic_move_change` instead of asking users to unlink then link manually.

Inputs:

- `from_epic_id`
- `to_epic_id`
- `change_id`
- `from_entry_id?`
- `target_path?`
- `order?`
- `moved_by?`
- `move_evidence` required
- `expected_from_version?`
- `expected_to_version?`

Behavior:

1. Validate source and destination Epics.
2. Validate child membership matches source Epic.
3. Add destination entry as `projection_pending`.
4. Update child projection to destination Epic.
5. Mark destination `linked`.
6. Remove or mark source entry `unlinked`.
7. On partial failure, statuses show exact recovery state; retry is idempotent by deterministic key `{from,to,change,entry}`.

### Repair Projection

Add `adv_epic_repair_membership` for stale cross-project/member projection recovery.

Modes:

- `sync_child_projection`: set/refresh child projection from Epic entry
- `clear_stale_projection`: clear child projection when Epic no longer owns entry
- `mark_target_unreachable`: update Epic entry status after failed target contact
- `dryRun` supported

Requires evidence and target confirmation when cross-project.

## Idempotency Requirement

Current `store-temporal/epics.ts` builds idempotency keys with `new Date().toISOString()`, making every retry unique. Revised link/unlink/move/promotion operations must use deterministic operation keys derived from semantic identity:

- link: `link|{epicId}|{entryId}|{projectId}|{changeId}`
- unlink: `unlink|{epicId}|{entryId}|{projectId}|{changeId}`
- move: `move|{fromEpic}|{toEpic}|{entryId}|{projectId}|{changeId}`
- promote: `promote|{epicId}|{shellEntryId}|{changeId}`

Timestamps remain payload/audit fields, not idempotency identity.

## Store/Workflow Changes

### Epic Workflow

Add signals:

- `changeLinkRequested` or extend `changeLinked` payload with `change_ref`, audit, and projection status
- `changeProjectionStatusUpdated`
- `changeMovedIn`
- `changeMovedOut`
- `changeUnlinked` extended with audit metadata

Add state:

- `epic_scope`
- entry `change_ref`
- entry `membership_status`
- audit ledger ring buffer for link/unlink/move/repair events

### Change Workflow

Add signals:

- `epicMembershipSetSignal(payload)`
- `epicMembershipClearedSignal(payload)`

Signal handlers validate:

- cannot set membership to a different Epic unless `source: 'move'` and prior membership matches expected source
- clear only if expected `{epic_id, entry_id}` matches
- fail closed when target read is unavailable and the operation needs to prove one-Epic-per-change; do not create a full `linked` entry without checking existing projection
- update search attributes after mutation through existing change-state projection path

Use `fireSignalAndRefresh` for tool-layer mutations tied to a change.

### Visibility

- `AdvEpicId` remains single-value Keyword from child projection.
- Same-project member query remains fast via Visibility.
- Cross-project Epic show does not rely on one project’s Visibility for all repos. It uses Epic entries as source-of-truth and optionally queries target projects for enrichment.

## Cross-Project/Product Semantics

### Ownership

An Epic has one owner project/workflow. For product Epics, owner should be the repo where the Epic was created, usually product primary repo.

### Membership

A product Epic can contain entries whose `change_ref.project_id` differs from owner project. The owner Epic entry is canonical for ordering and narrative; the child project projection is canonical for local change context.

### Safety

Cross-project membership mutation must route through `withTargetPathStore` with `stateRequirement: 'temporal-required'` and trust/confirmation rules:

- trusted related repo: proceed
- untrusted path: require `target_confirmed: true` and nonblank `confirmationEvidence`
- target queue not serviceable: fail before target mutation; Epic entry may be absent unless explicit `allow_unreachable_entry` is introduced later

### Unreachable Targets

Epic show/list must tolerate unreachable target projects:

```ts
member_status: {
  status: 'ok' | 'stale' | 'target_unreachable' | 'projection_missing' | 'projection_mismatch';
  last_checked_at: string;
  message?: string;
}
```

Default Epic view includes compact status, not stack traces. Full view can include details.

## Error Model

Add deterministic codes:

- `EPIC_NOT_FOUND`
- `CHANGE_NOT_FOUND`
- `CHANGE_ALREADY_IN_EPIC`
- `ENTRY_ALREADY_EXISTS`
- `ENTRY_NOT_FOUND`
- `PROJECTION_MISSING`
- `PROJECTION_MISMATCH`
- `TARGET_UNREACHABLE`
- `TARGET_CONFIRMATION_REQUIRED`
- `STALE_EPIC_VERSION`
- `PARTIAL_LINK_PENDING_REPAIR`
- `CROSS_PROJECT_SCOPE_REJECTED`

All mutation tools support `dryRun` where practical and return same-shape preview without signals.

## Migration Plan from Current Implementation

1. Extend schemas and specs first.
2. Keep existing repo-local Epic APIs backward-compatible.
3. Add parse/normalization tests for legacy `change_id` Epic entries and new `change_ref` entries.
4. Upgrade `adv_epic_link_change` to project membership onto same-project child changes.
5. Add same-project unlink/move and repair tests.
6. Add deterministic idempotency keys for Epic mutation tools/store operations.
7. Add cross-project `target_path` routing and product scope metadata.
8. Add multi-project Epic show enrichment and unreachable status.
9. Update command/agent docs and tool allowlists.
10. Re-run asset tests, targeted Epic tests, full test suite, check, and build.

## Acceptance Verification Strategy

- Unit tests: schema parsing, one-Epic-per-change, link/unlink/move pure state transitions, projection mismatch errors.
- Temporal tests: Epic workflow signals, change workflow membership set/clear, search attribute updates, continue-as-new preservation.
- Tool tests: `adv_epic_link_change`, `adv_epic_unlink_change`, `adv_epic_move_change`, repair dry-run/run, same-project and cross-project paths.
- Product tests: product Epic can reference at least two configured repo/project IDs.
- Context tests: `adv_change_show` and status snapshots show compact membership.
- Failure tests: target unavailable, untrusted target, duplicate link, stale version, projection mismatch.
- Regression: existing non-Epic changes and create-first shell promotion flow remain green.

## Independent Validator Result

Validator verdict: CAUTION, proceed with conditions. Required planning items:

1. Replace time-varying Epic idempotency keys with deterministic semantic keys.
2. Preserve backward compatibility for current `change_id` entries while introducing `change_ref`.
3. Route child projection set/clear through change signal + cache-refresh discipline.
4. Add audited unlink/move evidence and optimistic-concurrency checks.
5. Define fail-closed behavior for one-Epic-per-change when target read is unavailable.

## Design Risks

1. Distributed mutation cannot be truly atomic across Epic and child workflows. Mitigation: explicit membership statuses, idempotency keys, and repair tool.
2. Cross-project enrichment could become slow. Mitigation: default bounded view uses Epic entry state; target reads are opt-in or summarized with timeout/failure isolation.
3. Product scope may conflict with existing `scope_repos`. Mitigation: reuse repo identity shape but do not conflate change release scope with Epic membership scope.
4. One-Epic-per-change may be limiting. Keep V1 single for context clarity; future multi-membership would need `AdvEpicIds` KeywordList or different index strategy.

## Design Outcome

Proceed with revised implementation. The current code must not be accepted as complete until existing-change link/unlink/move and product/multi-project membership are structurally implemented, tested, and surfaced in Epic/change views.
documents.executiveSummary:
# Executive Summary

## Outcome

Advance now has first-class Epics for initiative-level planning: optional Epic containers, shell entries, child change membership projection, Temporal/Visibility support, MCP tools, bounded context surfaces, and audited retrofit/product-scope membership. Acceptance review found no remaining blockers or issues after reviewer remediation.

## Verdict

APPROVED

## What Was Built

1. Added typed Advance Epics specs, docs, Zod schemas, and public schema integration while keeping `epic_membership` optional on changes.
2. Added per-Epic Temporal workflow/runtime, pure state reducers, idempotency/rejection handling, workflow-safe boundaries, and store operations.
3. Added child `epic_membership` projection plus `AdvEpicId` single Keyword visibility support.
4. Added Epic MCP tools for create/show/list/update/reorder, shell add/promote, retroactive link/unlink/move, and membership repair.
5. Added product/multi-project Epic membership metadata and target-path trust routing for cross-project child projection mutation.
6. Added bounded Epic/change/status/CLI context surfaces, compact `member_status`, command/agent guidance, and spec/asset contracts.
7. Review remediation fixed update/reorder idempotency keys, stale rejection filtering, durable unlink/move audit payloads, duplicate change-entry guard, and link retry projection repair.

## What Was Verified

- Verdict: READY with 0 blockers and 0 issues remaining.
- Tests: targeted Epic suite passed after review fixes (73 tests); `pnpm run check` passed; exact `workflows.signal-handlers` rerun passed after one full-suite timeout; final `bin/oc-test full` rerun passed.
- Preview URL: not_applicable — backend/plugin/tooling change with no user-facing visual surface or browser UI output.
- Contract matrix: 16/16 required rows passed/respected; 0 failed/violated/unknown.

## Remaining Concerns

None.
